title: From Classical Filters to Learned Kernels
concept: Sobel's filter numbers were fixed once by an engineer, but a CNN's filter starts as random numbers; backpropagation then nudges each one after every training example until the filter learns to detect something useful.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What does a convolution compute at each position as a filter slides over an image?

options.text: A weighted sum — multiply the filter numbers by the pixels underneath, then add the results
The brightest pixel in that position
A count of how many pixels are pure white

exercises.explain: Right. Now let's trace where that filter's numbers actually come from.

cards.title: Where do the filter's numbers come from?
cards.body: Last lesson you saw that a convolution filter is just a small grid of numbers, learned instead of hand-picked. But how does a filter actually learn? The answer is something you've already met: backpropagation.

cards.title: Fixed numbers vs. learned numbers
cards.body: A classical Sobel filter's numbers, like -1, 0, and 1, were chosen once by an engineer and never change. A CNN's filter starts as small random numbers and updates them during training — the final values are learned, not designed.

cards.title: Learning to aim, one nudge at a time
cards.body: Imagine throwing darts blindfolded. Your first random throws mostly miss. But after each throw, you nudge your aim slightly based on how far off you were. A CNN filter starts random and gets nudged the same way, one training example at a time.

cards.title: Backprop nudges the filter
cards.body: Recall backpropagation from Units 71-72: the network makes a prediction, measures the error against the loss, and sends a correction signal backward through every weight. A convolution filter's numbers ARE weights — so backprop nudges them too, slightly adjusting each one to reduce the error a little.

cards.title: It isn't random guessing
cards.body: You might picture a computer randomly trying number combinations until one works. That's not it — gradient descent, guided by backprop, calculates exactly which direction to nudge each filter number to reduce error, and repeats this systematically across thousands of images.

cards.title: Sobel vs. CNN filter: where the numbers come from
cards.body: Same 3x3 grid shape, a completely different origin story:

left.text:
Numbers picked once
by a human engineer

right.text:
Starts random, nudged
by backprop every training step

exercises.prompt: What does a CNN's filter start out as, before any training happens?

options.text: The exact same numbers as a Sobel filter
Numbers copied from a finished network

exercises.explain: Right — every filter starts as noise and training shapes it into something useful.

exercises.prompt: True or false: backpropagation, the process you learned in Units 71-72, is the same mechanism that updates a CNN filter's numbers during training.
exercises.explain: True. A filter's numbers are just weights, and backprop updates weights of every kind, including these.

exercises.sentence: A Sobel filter's numbers are chosen once by an engineer and stay fixed; a CNN filter's numbers start random and are ___ by backpropagation during training.
exercises.explain: Nudged is right — each training example shifts the numbers a little closer to something useful.

exercises.prompt: Which statement correctly explains how a CNN filter's numbers become useful?

options.text: They start random and backpropagation gradually adjusts them based on prediction error until they detect something useful
An engineer manually tunes each number by hand, like Sobel
The numbers are copied from a lookup table of premade filters and never change

exercises.explain: Exactly — no hand-tuning involved, just repeated small corrections from backprop.

exercises.prompt: A brand-new CNN filter starts as random noise. After training on 10,000 images, that same filter reliably lights up on diagonal edges. What made that change happen?

options.text: Backpropagation repeatedly nudged the filter's numbers based on prediction error until they converged on a useful pattern
A human engineer manually replaced the random numbers with Sobel's numbers
The filter was randomly reshuffled until one arrangement happened to work
The image file format was converted, which reset the filter

exercises.explain: That's the mechanism! Next up: let's actually crunch the numbers for one position of a convolution, step by step.
